speaker: interviewer
text: the 5th of April, 1995. And we're in Sydney, Australia. Camera's rolling, and camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so you had a choice whether to stay or go?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was no choice. There was no choice. I mean, what they-- we knew if you stay behind, you get blown up. And I'm fortunate, fortunate, it didn't happen. And then we start marching to nowhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time? Was it day or night that you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Day. Now you asked the question, how many days did we march? When did we march? Where did we go to?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did we walk in day? Did we walk at nighttime? How were we treated?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't hardly can tell you. We walked day and night. We rest, and we walked. And we walked with thousands, countless. We always had-- we always have friends, people with us. We don't go in front. And we don't go in the back. We go in the center. Don't go too far in front. Don't go back in the back. I don't know how many walked. I think the Russians were very close-- must have been very close to us. And we started walking in the snow. It was February-- no, January.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what sort of clothing did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's nothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Camp clothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- you know, but we had plenty clothing after, because everyone who couldn't anymore and died-- they took-- someone died, someone fell, and what they had on, you took. And that's how it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So would you actually stop and pick up their clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you could stop anytime you want to. You could go back, too. The SS was around--

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you mean we could back?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, back-- you-- you fell off, you're walking-- it's not walking. You go slow, 10 kilometers a day, 20 a day. I don't know. How many do you go? We walked-- and I must tell you one thing. We walked for maybe a day. And there was suddenly on the side of the road, women. And they were shouting, Hollanders! Hollanders! It was my sister-in-law. I had one [NON-ENGLISH], she came back, too. She's still there-- she's still here. I met her on the way, a second, just hug, and go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did the SS see you do it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the SS was not everywhere. I mean, they-- we walked with thousands. There were not thousands of SS. There were-- now and again, they had push bikes, and they had motor bikes, and then on the side.

speaker: interviewer
text: You see, some of them escaped. But if you don't speak the language-- where-- we thought we go. You know, you could-- there was a chance to get out. But where can we go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: The police were so frightened, they wouldn't take you. They would be shot, themself. If you speak the language-- if you were Polish and you can speak the language, you had a much better chance. We couldn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so why was your sister-in-law there? How did she get to be there?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: You sister-in-law?

speaker: interviewee
text: She walked the same as me.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was walking, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also the same, she walked the same direction, and then they split up. And we kept on walking-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know, six, seven days, no shoes. Our shoes was just a piece of wood, and this-- how you call it? You know how shoes-- where those camp shoes. They were not normal shoes. And we kept on walking, and going, and cold, and freezing. And I still walk through it. And I never-- I never had problems with my feet. I know some people that lost their toes. And well, they fell on the wayside left and right. I mean, I don't know how many died. I would say 50% never made it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of state were you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Daze, but determined, because we thought, the war is finished. We are there. We made it. I was still only 23. You see? I was 23-- 23 or 43 makes a hell of a lot of difference. You're very strong, and will power. Hate. I don't know how many people you talk to-- they must all be now in the early 70s. They survived. And they were only in their early 20s-- 20-- 19, 18, 20. The ones from 30, they're not many of them who survived. You know, the ones who were 40, they never made it. They never made it, couldn't. And we kept on going, going, going. I remember one boy, he lost his mind. And he had to-- we had a piece of rope. And we dragged him from a few days, still along. He chugged along, chugged along.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you mean he lost his mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was gone. He was gone-- nothing, no, no, absolutely no comprehension at all, nothing. And no food, there was nothing there. And now and again, rest on the road for an hour maybe, sitting in the snow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you fall asleep when you stopped?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you never slept. You never slept in the camp, really. No-- a doze, maybe. And you were in a doze-- a daze. You didn't talk. You just keep on going, chugging, walking, walking.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was no difference between sleeping and waking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: No difference between sleeping--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I wouldn't say so. You were in a daze. No, I wouldn't say so.

speaker: interviewer
text: It is indescribable. It really is. I think back. How did I survive? It was so long ago. I don't know. How did one person survive? We don't know. It's unbelievable. Do you remember, did you dream at all? Do you remember dreaming or anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now?

speaker: interviewer
text: In camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dream? You never have a chance to [LAUGHS] You couldn't-- you never slept proper. You never slept.

speaker: interviewer
text: How could you sleep? You had in one bed, you had four people. You had another pair of legs from this side, and then next to you. And, no, you couldn't ever sleep-- straw, dirty, filthy, no.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we kept on walking, the march-- walking, walking, walking. Till one day, suddenly we came somewhere, was a train. The last day, one day in the train, open wagon. Maybe I wouldn't have made it. And the train went up till Gross-Rosen-- the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean, maybe you wouldn't have made it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I mean, I had one day-- I saved one day walking, really. Instead of walking eight or nine days, I walked maybe six or seven days. I don't know the time. And one night and one day, we went in the train. And those trains don't go very fast. I was just lucky that maybe that part of my transport they could push in a train. And we finished in Gross-Rosen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you still with some friends at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we all had a few together. You know, yes, not many are alive. And-- and then we arrived in Gross-Rosen. And there were thousands already. They came from everywhere. And all those-- there are so many camps. The [NON-ENGLISH] and little [NON-ENGLISH] and lagers-- unbelievable. Thousands of them. And there was no room. And Gross-Rosen was not in-- was a very bad camp, [NON-ENGLISH]. And maybe the accommodation was 30,000. There were maybe 100,000, maybe more. I don't know. Countless-- three times as many as the capacity of the camp. There was no food. They gave us a little bit of nothing. And we thought, well, laying in the mud outside one of those barracks there-- not a barrack, you know, of the working parts of it, they were laying. And we slept. And I was there maybe a day, maybe two days, a day or two days. And, suddenly, we have to go in transport again. They round us up. They hit us up. And we walk to the train again. We didn't know where to go. We didn't have a clue.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you arrived there, did you think that might have been the end?

speaker: interviewee
text: I thought so. Then we thought we're going. We-- we thought they shoot us. And then the trains were standing there. And I was one of the first in the wagon. It was a closed wagon. And I sit down, you know, nicely on the side there until there were another 10, 15, 20 in, and then another 20 in, and another 20 in, and another 20 in. And then we have to stand up. We couldn't sit anymore. And then end of the story, we're standing like this. 130-- this documented. I know more about it. And we were at 130 men in the wagon. And the train start rolling. There were some open wagons. We had a closed wagon. It's good for six horses. It was written on it, six [NON-ENGLISH], it was written on it. You know what room is six horses-- 130 men. And we start rolling. And we did that for six days and seven nights [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] till we arrived in-- the train goes backwards and forwards, backwards and forwards, and backwards. Well, after one day, there was a little bit more room because the people start dying. We lay them down. And when we arrived in Dachau, there were 29 left of the 130. We were sitting right on top-- right on top of the bodies, because everyone who died, down, we laid them down. And we sit on top of them. Filthy, dirty, the toilets, you know, they-- we ripped clothes off-- the smell-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] must have been something. It must have been a sight for us, how we looked like. And I didn't look at myself in those days. And I survived. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see yourself, ever see a reflection of yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I saw the other ones. I remember one fellow who I spoke to, he was as flat as a pancake, because everything-- he rips, everything goes cracks. If you stand on it, and lay on it, with no-- and you put them up. You've seen those pictures of those. That's how it was. And I don't know how I survived there, no idea. So in other words, I nearly went from Auschwitz to Dachau all in-- was about two weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's where you ended up on that train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dachau. I would say it was about a week. You know, there's documented, seven days. It was more than a week, a week, or eight days, or seven days, give me a day-- give and take a day. It's quite a distance. And the trains didn't go backwards, and forwards, and stop, and then-- and we couldn't get out of it, because the train was locked. The only thing is we could take-- there were little bit windows. And we could tug a little bit snow. There was all snow in those days, but no. Some try to drink their own urine. It's-- it was thirst. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people try and support each other in it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was no support. How can you support? There was nothing to support anyone. You stay alive. And-- and I did stay alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]. Worry. It's OK. You're fine. Don't worry. Keep going.

speaker: interviewee
text: Look at him.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK. It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in Dachau--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so you arrived, so you finally arrived.

speaker: interviewee
text: I arrived in Dachau. And I must have had something. In Dachau, when we arrived on that railway station, this I remember, even SS collapsed when they saw us coming out of the train. When they opened the wagons, they collapsed. There were [NON-ENGLISH] and women standing there. When they saw us, they just fainted. We must have been a sight-- [INAUDIBLE] and nude. And I also remember when we walked from Dachau railway station to camp-- I don't have a distance, the SS walked on the side with a machine gun against the houses that no one must look out of the window. And warning if they looked, they shoot. So we must have been a sight, how we came out of it. And the other story, I was dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were dead?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 95% dead. And I arrived in Dachau. And there I had a shower, the best shower I ever had in my life. [LAUGHS] I never forget. I must tell that. And then I-- the doctors were sitting there on tables, the camp doctors. And they pushed me to a table.

speaker: interviewee
text: And who sits there? A Dutchman-- non-Jewish doctor, who I met in Furth. And we helped him, because he was already in the 40s, and he was not strong. And we helped him a little bit in Furth, what we could.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he was not a doctor in camp-- in Furth camp. Apparently, from there he went to Dachau. And there, he became a camp doctor. And I came to his table. And I recognized him. He didn't recognize me. And his name was Jan. I said, Jan. And he said to me, shut up, in Dutch, [SPEAKING DUTCH]. That's all. And he went to me. He said, typhus. As soon as you have typhus, the SS was frightened for it. Dachau was not a destroying lager-- no gas chambers. But you die there. And they had a few typhus barracks. And all the sick ones went in those barracks. And they were laying there. And they died by the hundreds. It's countless.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in that barrack, they had a few beds on one side. And they put a few healthy ones-- very risky, too. You know, there might be some infection. But he kept me for 12 days-- 12 days. You know what that means, 12 days? Laying in bed there and getting to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: I recovered completely. I wouldn't say I was healthy. But I had 12 days food and resting. That saved my life. The man saved my life. I've seen him after the war-- he's not alive anymore. He's a very big underground worker, that man, in Rotterdam, non-Jewish doctor. And-- and he saved my life.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is fate. I mean, it's like you say, yes. But why do I have to go to his table? If I would have gone to the next table, I wouldn't have been alive today.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's with all of us. We all have something-- like I say, yes, that is up there. No, it's luck. I mean, it's-- it's like a lottery. And I was in that barrack for 12 days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when you first recognized him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I recognized him immediately. Oh, I did-- I didn't-- they didn't-- I walked to the table. They sent me to the table. You know, they said, two here, two there, four here, you know, and table by table. I recognized him. He looked good. He was a doctor there. He had quite a reasonable existence there.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wouldn't say he had a good life, but better than-- if you're a doctor in-- in a camp, you always have a little bit better off. Even a painter was a little bit better off, you know? So you were in a daze most of the time. And at that stage, you were really, really--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so when you saw him, was there a different spark in you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Jan, I could say to him. How are you? Jan-- you know, yes, I did. He sparked me more-- amazing. He didn't recognize me, he told me later. But he knew that I was one from Furth. He knew. Otherwise, I wouldn't have known him. And he only said to me in Dutch, shut up, because the SS was walking around, standing around. And most of them died in camp, most of-- from my transport. Not many came out of the wagon. We came out with 29, I think. And-- and I've never seen one back, really. Only one, one fellow from Holland. He's still alive. He wrote me. I wrote him, and he wrote me back. He's 81 years now. He was much older than I. He's still alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow. And so you're in bed for 12 days. And what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the 12, he couldn't keep me there. I say 12 days, it could be-- I guess. And then I have to get out. And they smuggled me out of the barrack. I supposed to be dead, of course. They did not know names anymore. And he put me in a barrack that goes on transport the next day. You know, there were transport barracks. And they select up straight away, five here, 10 there, [NON-ENGLISH]. And I went to Muhldorf. And I arrived at Muhldorf, which is not far-- e0-- 30 kilometers from Dachau. And in Muhldorf, straight away they sent me to Ampfing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the transports like there? How many people were you with then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you stand up in-- in a truck. There's a truck that is-- you don't even sit. There's open truck, you know. And 20, 30 one wagon, you know. SS sort of [NON-ENGLISH], that they transport you. They can't see you this high up. Even if you stand up, they can't see you. They put very high. And standing, or sitting, or hanging on. It was only short distance, onl only only 25, 30 kilometers, no more. Yeah, it was-- I don't even-- I've done it. I've been back. Approximately, I would say 25 kilometers. And then I went to Ampfing. I was in a very bad lager in Ampfing. And there was nothing, anymore. It was already-- it was-- I arrived in Ampfing in March. It must have been in March. February, it must be March. And it was cement works there. And they were carrying bags of cement up-- walked up. I've seen it. But I've never done it, because there was nothing there. There was no cement. There was nothing there. And I was in Ampfing maybe one day.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't quite understand.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Ampfing, it was in very bad lager, and they were carrying bags of cement. That was the main work there-- up to-- up a hill, this up. And no one made it. They say one, two, three days, they were gone. It was terrible there. But I never made it, because the next day, I don't know what commander want to give us. The next day there was-- oh, I tell you that, the-- he bombs were throwing around left and right. There was an enormous attack from the Americans. In Muhldorf is a railway crossing, apparently. And the whole-- the whole camp had to go to Muhldorf railway crossing to clean the rails and the wagons. And that took weeks. You know, every day there, and-- and picking up what was there, and shifting, and you know-- make the roads clear for-- for that the train could run again, roll again. And it took a week, what it was. And we cleaned it up nicely. We didn't do it too fast. I even found an American in-- I remember when we walked to the railway station, we found an American body in-- that was a shot from a plane. And we found a whole American. And we took his cigarettes, and his belt, and what-- and what we could get from him. I mean, there was no feeling. He was dead. And I had a little bit. I had a few pack of cigarettes, and could deal with it. You could deal with the outside, you know, even with-- I must say, the SS start disappearing. It became home guards, old men. They were also SS, or Wehrmacht. And they were just as frightened as we were, really. They were not as bad, because I remember we walked once out of camp. And we pinched potatoes. And if that would have happened in other camp, in Auschwitz, they would have shot you. And we had them here. We had the potatoes in here. And we made it loose. And the potatoes roll out. And they hit you on your head, bang, not hard. They didn't hit, and were in the camp. And then I worked in Ampfing-- I worked in Ampfing it must be six, seven weeks. But every time when the rail-- when the rails were fixed, they bombed them again-- [LAUGHS] three, four times. Every time, they were not-- it was not anymore defense. They lay the bombs down. And I talk about end of the war, really. That was the end of the war. The Americans were over there every day by the-- with the airplanes. I don't know what they bombed, Muhldorf, the railway. They went up to Austria, I don't know where. But planes day and night, you could see them going, low. There was no defense.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was the feeling then? Was it like--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that we knew. We knew it was end. Oh yes, we knew. We didn't know when.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did the end come?

speaker: interviewee
text: We walked to camp. But there was nothing to eat. Every day, we hoping and hoping. We knew it is nearly finished, but not knowing when. And that went on. I must have been in Ampfing [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a month or more-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] March-- six weeks, five, six weeks. I was only working on that railway, more or less, only cleaning the rails, cleaning the rails every day. And then bombed again, and cleaning it again. And it was a lot of work. It was heavy, you know, with lifting up rails. You know, what a mess to make out of it if you put a few bombs on it. Big holes in the ground, make the ground, make it even with shovels, and doing. And then, you know, that work. And then one day, it must've been very late in April, suddenly our commander-- appel, transport. We went in the transport again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did we go to? We're in transport, end of the war? I found out after the war where the transport was to. We went in a train-- not as bad, open wagon. It was not so cold. It was not that cold.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was May, early May, or later. And we went in the train. The train would move forward and backward. It was only 30 kilometers from Munich. We did maybe 40 kilometers in two days, hardly. The train was standing still on the fields, middle in the fields. And some boys, they went to the villages close by. And-- and they were shot by straying SS. And I had a friend with me. He say, we stay-- if we are liberated, we stay in the train. And we did. And this train start moving again. I remember daytime. And we moved again. And at night time, we must have passed Dachau that way, or Munich again, because Muhldorf, Muhldorf, Dachau. We must pass somewhere. And at night time, the train was standing still. And, suddenly, the Americans came over the plane and machine gunned the train. A few were killed. A friend of mine was killed, and doctor. And the-- the engine was hit. And the plane couldn't get further anymore. And we were standing in nowhere, waiting.

speaker: interviewer
text: They open the wagons. Who is they?

speaker: interviewee
text: The SS. It was not even SS anymore, open the wagons. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] early in the morning, suddenly a lot of noise, tanks, Americans. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: We all on the railway station, on the-- on the-- you know, how you call it? It was a normal station, [NON-ENGLISH]. We were standing there, [NON-ENGLISH]. I never forget the name, [NON-ENGLISH]. And there, we were liberated by the Americans. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: The American soldiers, they-- they addressed the troops from General Patton. They were flabbergasted when they-- what they have seen. They couldn't believe it. And, of course, the big mistake they made is the canned food, give us food. That's the biggest mistake they made.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so did you purposely not take some?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't. I didn't. I knew that. I mean, that's-- I had a friend who was a doctor. He died the day before. No, he was not my friend. But we traveled quite a while together from-- he also came from Auschwitz, a Dutch-- a Dutch fellow. And then on top of a tank, came an American officer, high officer, in Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, don't touch food. Don't come near anything-- in Yiddish. You're free. He surrounded the whole railway station. There were thousands of them, I mean-- matter of fact, we were in [NON-ENGLISH], must also have been in [NON-ENGLISH] or Feldafing-- you see the train in different parts. There are more trains, apparently. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the Americans came virtually immediately-- I mean, half an hour later-- with the Germans use that little wagon on two wheels, and they made straightaway food for us, water, and milk were mixed here a little bit of-- of-- and they gave us something to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, that's the end of tape four of the interview with Samuel Lissing. It's the 5th of April, 1995. We are in Sydney, Australia.
